reference: Hello, no, this is not a heart attack. This can be a cardiac arrhythmia, such as paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia or atrial fibrillation, in which the heart rate suddenly goes up and also suddenly decreases. Blood pressure falls during these episodes, and one may have chest pain, dizziness, fainting, blackouts, and sometimes syncope. Usually, the electrocardiogram is normal, as it is conducted between the episodes. An electrocardiogram done during episodes will show abnormalities. You will need twenty-four-hour Holter monitoring if you are having episodes on a daily basis; otherwise, you should rush to the hospital or at least measure your pulse rate during the episode. So, visit a nearby cardiologist for these investigations. Also, hemoglobin and thyroid tests should be done. In order to abort such episodes, you should try to cough hard. Is there any stress or anxiety associated? Until your evaluation is complete, you can ask your local doctor for tablet Clopidrogrel twenty milligrams once a day. If none of the above is successful, then the last option is electrophysiological studies, which is an invasive test that can diagnose and treat the arrhythmia permanently in most cases.
text: Hello, no, this is not a heart attack. This can be a cardiac arrhythmia such as paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia or atrial fibrillation in which the heart rate suddenly goes up and also suddenly decreases. Blood pressure falls during these episodes and one may have chest pain, dizziness, fainting, blackouts and sometimes sycopy. Usually the electrocardiogram is normal as it is conducted between the episodes. An electrocardiogram done during episodes will show abnormalities. You will need 24-hour Holter monitoring if you are having episodes on a daily basis. Otherwise, you should rush to the hospital or at least measure your pulse rate during the episode. So, visit a nearby cardiologist for these investigations. Also, hemoglobin and thyroid tests should be done. In order to abort such episodes, you should try to cough hard. Is there any stress or anxiety associated? Until your evaluation is complete, you can ask your local doctor for care. For example, you can take a tablet called Pitogrel 20 mg once a day. If none of the above is successful, then the last option is electrophysiological studies which is an invasive test that can diagnose and treat the arrhythmia permanently in most cases.
